_scenario: Baby 6 months — All domains on-track. Enrichment plan, activities spread evenly across all 3 domains. Actual LLM content will vary; structure must match exactly.
child_summary: This 7-day enrichment plan is for your 6-month-old in LEARN mode. All three domains — Motor Development (78.0), Communication (80.5), and Social Engagement (76.0) — are on-track. Activities are spread evenly across all domains to nurture well-rounded development with age-appropriate enrichment that parents can easily do at home.

activities.title: Supported Sitting Play
activities.description: Prop baby in a supported sit and offer a range of textured toys to explore.
steps: Prop baby in a supported sit and offer a range of textured toys to explore.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Mirror Time
activities.description: Hold baby in front of a mirror and name body parts they touch.
steps: Hold baby in front of a mirror and name body parts they touch.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Singing Routine
activities.description: Sing the same 2 songs during bath and meal time to build anticipation.
steps: Sing the same 2 songs during bath and meal time to build anticipation.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Rolling Practice — Belly to Back
activities.description: From tummy time, gently guide baby's hip to initiate a roll onto their back.
steps: Place baby on their tummy on a firm mat.
Hold a bright toy to their right side at eye level.
Place your left hand gently on their right hip.
Nudge the hip forward slowly so baby rolls onto their back.
Celebrate with a big smile and repeat on the other side.
activities.tip: Rolling from tummy to back is a key 6-month milestone — guiding the motion teaches baby the movement pattern.

activities.title: Two-Sound Conversation
activities.description: Make two different sounds — 'ba' and 'ma' — and pause between each, inviting baby to respond.
steps: Hold baby face-to-face at arm's length.
Say 'ba ba ba' slowly and clearly, then pause 5 seconds.
Say 'ma ma ma' and pause 5 seconds.
When baby vocalizes — any sound — immediately copy them back.
Do 3 rounds of each sound.
activities.tip: Responding to baby's vocalizations teaches them that their sounds have power — the very foundation of language.

activities.title: Lap Bounce Song
activities.description: Bounce baby gently on your lap in rhythm with a simple song — stop mid-song and wait for baby to react.
steps: Sit baby on your lap facing you, supported at the waist.
Start bouncing gently in rhythm with a song ('Humpty Dumpty' works well).
Stop suddenly in the middle and make an expectant face.
Wait 5 seconds — baby will often bounce themselves or make a sound for 'more'.
Resume the song and repeat the pause 3 times.
activities.tip: Anticipation of a predictable pattern — and reacting to the gap — is one of the earliest social-cognitive skills.

activities.title: Texture Basket Explore
activities.description: Fill a small basket with household items of different textures and let baby pull them out one by one.
steps: Place 4 items of very different textures in a basket — e.g. a wooden spoon, a soft cloth, a plastic lid, a crinkle paper.
Sit baby in a supported position with the basket in front.
Let them reach in and pull out any item.
Name the texture as they touch it: 'smooth', 'soft', 'crinkly'.
When they drop it, let them choose the next one.
activities.tip: Tactile exploration at 6 months directly stimulates the sensory pathways that feed into fine motor control and body awareness.

activities.title: Story Sound Effects
activities.description: Read a picture book but replace some words with sounds — animal noises, whoosh, pop — and pause for baby to react.
steps: Open a simple picture book with clear images.
Point to a dog and say 'woof woof!' instead of 'dog'.
Pause and look at baby expectantly.
When they respond (smile, sound, wiggle), nod and turn the page.
Do 5 pages with a different sound on each.
activities.tip: Sound effects during stories make reading interactive — baby's responses teach them that they are part of the conversation.

activities.title: Pass the Toy
activities.description: Hold a toy out to baby, let them take it, then hold your hand out and wait for them to hand it back.
steps: Sit facing baby with a bright toy in your hand.
Hold it out toward them and say 'for you!'.
Let baby take and hold the toy for 5 seconds.
Hold your open hand out and say 'my turn?'.
Wait up to 10 seconds for baby to release or hand over the toy.
Repeat 4 times.
activities.tip: The earliest turn-taking with objects is the same skill that eventually becomes conversation — taking and giving.

activities.title: Tripod Sitting Practice
activities.description: Support baby in a wide-legged sit and let them use their hands on the floor in front as a tripod for balance.
steps: Sit baby on a firm surface with their legs in a wide V shape.
Place their hands on the floor in front for support.
Let go for 2 seconds and watch if they hold the tripod position.
Place a toy on the floor 10 cm in front to give them a focal point.
Build to 5-second holds over 3 rounds.
activities.tip: The tripod sit is the 6-month stage before independent sitting — core and arm muscles develop together in this position.

activities.title: Name That Object
activities.description: Slowly pull 3 common objects from a bag one at a time, name each one clearly, and let baby hold them.
steps: Place a spoon, a cup, and a cloth in a bag.
Pull out the spoon slowly and say 'spoon!' — hold it up clearly.
Let baby take it and hold for 5 seconds.
Take it back gently, put it aside, and pull out the next.
Repeat for all 3. On round 2, say 'where's the spoon?' and see if baby looks.
activities.tip: Hearing the same word repeatedly while holding and looking at the object creates lasting vocabulary links in baby's brain.

activities.title: Clap Along
activities.description: Hold baby's hands and clap them together in rhythm with a song — then stop and see if they try to clap.
steps: Sing 'If you're happy and you know it'.
During the clapping part, hold baby's hands and clap them together twice.
Repeat the verse 3 times.
On the 4th verse, let go and see if baby initiates any clapping movement.
Celebrate any arm movement as a clap.
activities.tip: Clapping is a bilateral motor skill and social signal — baby is learning that movement can be both fun and communicative.

activities.title: Midline Reach & Transfer
activities.description: Place a toy in baby's right hand and encourage them to pass it to their left hand across their midline.
steps: Lay baby on their back or sit them with support.
Place a small toy in their right hand.
Hold your open palm near their left hand invitingly.
Wait 10 seconds for baby to bring the toy across to the other hand.
If needed, gently guide the transfer. Repeat on the other side 3 times each.
activities.tip: Crossing the midline integrates both brain hemispheres — a foundational skill for reading, writing, and coordinated movement.

activities.title: Where Did It Go?
activities.description: Hide a toy under a cloth in full view of baby and ask 'where did it go?' — watch if they reach to uncover it.
steps: Sit baby facing you with a bright toy between you.
Make sure baby is looking, then cover the toy with a small cloth.
Say 'where did it go?' with a curious face.
Wait 5 seconds.
If baby doesn't reach, lift a corner of the cloth to prompt them.
Repeat 4 times — vary which cloth or hand you use.
activities.tip: Searching for a hidden object shows object permanence is developing — baby knows things exist even when they can't see them.

activities.title: Stranger Warm-Up
activities.description: Introduce baby to a new face gradually — caregiver holds baby while the new person approaches slowly.
steps: Hold baby securely facing outward.
Ask an unfamiliar person (or less-familiar family member) to stand 2 meters away.
Let the person make gentle eye contact and smile — no sudden moves.
After 30 seconds, they can take one slow step closer.
If baby is calm, the person can speak softly. Stop if baby shows distress.
activities.tip: Healthy stranger wariness at 6 months is normal — gradual exposure with a safe caregiver builds confident, flexible social skills.

activities.title: Supported Standing Bounce
activities.description: Hold baby upright so their feet touch a firm surface and let them push down and bounce.
steps: Hold baby upright under their arms so their feet are flat on a firm surface.
Let them feel the surface — most 6-month-olds will push down and bounce.
Allow the bouncing for 20 seconds, then let them rest.
Talk to them: 'bounce, bounce, bounce!' in rhythm.
Repeat 3 rounds.
activities.tip: Weight-bearing through the legs at 6 months builds the bone density and muscle tone that supports walking much later.

activities.title: Yes & No Face
activities.description: Make an exaggerated 'yes' face for things baby likes and a clear 'no, yucky' face for things they don't — teach early emotional vocabulary.
steps: Hold a preferred toy up and say 'do you like this?' with a big happy face.
Then hold up a less preferred item and say 'yucky?' with a funny disgusted face.
Watch baby's expression and mirror their reaction back.
Try 4 different items — 2 liked, 2 not liked.
Name the emotion each time: 'happy!' or 'no, yucky!'
activities.tip: Giving babies words for their reactions is the earliest emotional vocabulary — it helps them communicate preferences before they can speak.

activities.title: Blanket Swing
activities.description: Lay baby on a large blanket, hold each end with another adult, and gently swing side to side.
steps: Lay baby on a large firm blanket on the floor.
Each adult takes two corners of the blanket.
Gently lift to remove slack — baby should remain flat, not sag.
Slowly swing left and right 5 cm only, 3 times.
Lower, let baby settle, then do 2 more gentle swings.
activities.tip: The vestibular input from gentle swinging stimulates the balance system and often produces pure delight — shared laughter is powerful bonding.

activities.title: Unassisted Sit for 10 Seconds
activities.description: Remove all support and see if baby can hold an independent sit for 10 seconds with a toy as focal point.
steps: Sit baby on a firm mat with legs out and your hands lightly at their hips.
Place a toy on the mat 10 cm in front.
Slowly release your hands and count silently to 10.
If baby tips, catch them gently — no alarm.
Try 3 rounds, celebrating each one.
activities.tip: A 10-second unassisted sit is the Day 7 goal of this week's motor work — it shows the core muscles are integrating the training.

activities.title: My Day Story
activities.description: Narrate baby's whole day back to them in simple sentences at bedtime — 'we ate, we played, we bathed'.
steps: Hold baby in a calm, dim room at bedtime.
Speak slowly and quietly: 'today we woke up. then we had breakfast.'
Continue through the day's events in order.
Use the same 2-3 word phrases you said during each activity.
Finish with 'now it's time to sleep. good night.'
activities.tip: Narrative repetition at the end of the day strengthens memory links between words and experiences — building language and routine simultaneously.

activities.title: Hello & Goodbye Wave
activities.description: Teach the wave gesture by waving to baby and waiting for any arm movement in response.
steps: Hold baby facing another adult standing 1 meter away.
The adult waves slowly and says 'hello!'.
You take baby's hand and wave it back, saying 'hello!'.
The adult then moves behind a door and reappears: 'hello again!'.
Repeat 4 times — try removing your hand on the last 2 to see if baby waves unprompted.
activities.tip: Waving is one of the first communicative gestures — learning it means baby understands that body movement sends a message to another person.

general_tips: All domains are healthy — the goal this week is enrichment, not intervention. Keep activities joyful and pressure-free.
Six months is a peak time for forming attachment — your warm, responsive presence during every activity matters more than the activity itself.
Follow baby's lead on duration — if they look away or fuss, the session is done. Ending on a positive note is always better than pushing on.
Repeat activities baby responds well to across the week — repetition is how baby brains build strong neural pathways.
Talk constantly during all activities — narrate what you see, what you are doing, and what baby is doing. Every word counts.
